encounter_summary: You are on scene with a 58-year-old male who called 9-1-1 for crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the left arm, onset 40 minutes ago at rest. Your 12-lead ECG shows ST elevation in leads V1 through V4 consistent with an anterior STEMI. He is diaphoretic, pale, and increasingly anxious.

elements.label: 58-year-old male, crushing chest pain 40 minutes
ST elevation leads V1 through V4, anterior STEMI
Pain radiates to left arm, 9 out of 10
BP 102/68, HR 96, SpO2 94% on room air
Diaphoretic and pale, onset at rest
Aspirin 324 mg given, IV established
Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL given once
Pain decreased from 9 to 7 after nitro
History of hypertension, takes lisinopril daily
ETA 6 minutes, cath lab activation requested
Patient divorced, lives alone with two cats
Neighbor called 9-1-1 after hearing him yell

hint: Ask what the receiving team needs in the first minute: demographics, mechanism/complaint, key findings, interventions/response, and ETA. Social details only belong if they change care.

encounter_summary: You respond to a 4-year-old female in moderate respiratory distress. Her mother reports progressive wheeze and work of breathing over the past two hours that is not responding to home albuterol. The child is sitting upright and using accessory muscles; you can hear audible wheezing and see subcostal retractions. She has a history of asthma.

elements.label: 4-year-old female, known asthma, moderate distress
Wheeze and increased work of breathing two hours
SpO2 91% on room air, RR 34, HR 138
Subcostal retractions, audible wheeze bilaterally
No improvement with three home albuterol treatments
Albuterol 2.5 mg nebulized en route, O2 applied
SpO2 improved to 96% after nebulizer treatment
Allergic to amoxicillin per mother
ETA 4 minutes, pediatric team requested
Wheezing less prominent after treatment, still present
Child attends preschool, father works from home
Mother mentioned dog in home, possible new trigger

hint: Ask what the receiving team needs in the first minute: demographics, mechanism/complaint, key findings, interventions/response, and ETA. Social details only belong if they change care.

encounter_summary: You are treating a 34-year-old unbelted male driver following a high-speed T-bone collision with airbag deployment. On arrival he was alert but now has a GCS of 12, significant left flank pain, an unstable pelvis on exam, and progressively worsening hypotension despite two large-bore IVs. You are 11 minutes from the trauma center.

elements.label: 34-year-old male, unbelted driver, high-speed T-bone
Airbag deployed, significant intrusion driver door
BP 84/60 and dropping, HR 128, GCS 12
Unstable pelvis, left flank ecchymosis, guarding
Two large-bore IVs, warm normal saline running
Pelvic binder applied, C-spine precautions in place
BP trending down, 92 to 84 over 8 minutes
Decreased breath sounds left base, possible hemo
ETA 11 minutes, activate trauma team now
No significant past medical history per bystander
Other driver was cited at scene by police
Patient's car was totaled, airbags cost about $3,000

hint: Ask what the receiving team needs in the first minute: demographics, mechanism/complaint, key findings, interventions/response, and ETA. Social details only belong if they change care.

encounter_summary: You respond to a report of an unresponsive adult male found in a park restroom. On arrival the patient is a 29-year-old male, unresponsive and apneic, with a GCS of 3. Drug paraphernalia and a used syringe are found at the scene. Pupils are 1 mm bilaterally and he has slow, gurgling respirations that resolved after airway positioning.

elements.label: 29-year-old male, unresponsive, suspected opioid OD
Found unresponsive in restroom, syringe at scene
GCS 3, pupils 1 mm bilateral, RR 4 and agonal
SpO2 74% on arrival, improved with airway positioning
Naloxone 2 mg IN given on scene
BVM ventilations initiated before naloxone
Patient responsive to voice, RR 14 after naloxone
SpO2 98% on 15L NRB after airway management
ETA 5 minutes, repeat dose may be needed
Bystander says patient uses heroin regularly
Bystander mentioned patient recently lost his job
Police found an open warrant when running his name

hint: Ask what the receiving team needs in the first minute: demographics, mechanism/complaint, key findings, interventions/response, and ETA. Social details only belong if they change care.

encounter_summary: You are transporting a 4-year-old male with severe respiratory distress after two days of cough and fever. He is tripod-positioned with retractions, nasal flaring, and poor air movement. Initial SpO2 was 84% RA and improved to 93% after blow-by oxygen and assisted nebulized albuterol. HR 162, RR 44, BP 88/52. Parents report asthma and no known medication allergies.

elements.label: 4-year-old male, severe pediatric respiratory distress
Two days cough/fever, history of asthma
Tripod, retractions, nasal flaring, poor air movement
SpO2 84% RA improved to 93% with oxygen and nebulizer
Assisted albuterol nebulizer given with partial improvement
Parent states no known medication allergies
Considering CPAP/ALS intercept if work of breathing worsens
ETA 8 minutes to pediatric-capable ED
Child's favorite toy is a green dinosaur
Parents gave acetaminophen 4 hours ago
Family dog has also been coughing this week

hint: A respiratory handoff should lead with age, distress severity, oxygenation, work of breathing, interventions, response, escalation concerns, and ETA.

encounter_summary: You are bringing in a 67-year-old female with sudden right facial droop, right arm drift, and slurred speech. Last known well was 42 minutes ago while eating breakfast. BGL is 118 mg/dL. BP 184/96, HR 82, SpO2 97% RA. Cincinnati stroke screen is positive. No anticoagulant information is available yet.

elements.label: 67-year-old female, suspected acute stroke
Last known well 42 minutes ago
Right facial droop, right arm drift, slurred speech
Positive Cincinnati stroke screen
BGL 118 mg/dL
Stroke alert requested on arrival
No anticoagulant information available yet
ETA 6 minutes to stroke center
Breakfast was oatmeal and coffee
Family is following in a private vehicle
Patient normally gardens on weekends

hint: For stroke, the receiving team needs last-known-well, objective deficits, glucose, vitals, destination capability, and ETA.

encounter_summary: You respond to a 16-year-old female with sudden hives, facial swelling, wheezing, and dizziness after eating a cookie containing peanuts. BP is 82/48, HR 132, RR 30, SpO2 91% RA. She has a prescribed epinephrine auto-injector that was administered by EMS with improvement in wheezing and BP to 96/58.

elements.label: 16-year-old female, anaphylaxis after peanut exposure
Hives, facial swelling, wheezing, dizziness
Initial BP 82/48, HR 132, RR 30, SpO2 91% RA
Prescribed epinephrine auto-injector administered by EMS
Wheezing improved and BP rose to 96/58 after epinephrine
Oxygen applied and airway swelling monitored
Known peanut allergy
Second epinephrine dose may be needed if symptoms recur
ETA 4 minutes, requesting immediate room assignment
Cookie was from a school bake sale
Parent is bringing the epinephrine package
Patient has a math test tomorrow

hint: Anaphylaxis handoff should make the trigger, airway/breathing/circulation findings, epinephrine timing, response, recurrence risk, and ETA unmistakable.

encounter_summary: You are transporting a 44-year-old male from a head-on MVC. He was restrained with significant front-end intrusion. He has chest wall tenderness, abdominal guarding, pale cool skin, HR 128, BP 86/54, RR 26, SpO2 95% on oxygen. Pelvic binder applied, two large-bore IVs established by ALS, and rapid transport is underway.

elements.label: 44-year-old male, head-on MVC with major intrusion
Restrained driver, significant front-end intrusion
Chest wall tenderness and abdominal guarding
Pale cool skin, HR 128, BP 86/54
RR 26, SpO2 95% on oxygen
Pelvic binder applied for suspected pelvic injury
Two large-bore IVs established by ALS
Rapid transport to trauma center underway
ETA 7 minutes, trauma team activation requested
Airbags deployed
Vehicle color was blue
Patient was driving to work

hint: For trauma, lead with mechanism, suspected injuries, shock signs, life-saving interventions, destination/activation need, and ETA.
